_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic criteria from ISHAM ABPA Working Group (Agarwal et al. Clin Exp Allergy 2013, PMID 23889240) and updated 2024 consensus. Prevalence from Agarwal et al. Int J Tuberc Lung Dis 2009 systematic review (PMID 19723382): 2.5% in asthma, 7.8% in CF. Treatment trials including ISDIAB (oral prednisolone vs itraconazole). Biologic data on mepolizumab/dupilumab from case series. Stages per Patterson 1982 staging system.

heroOverview: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) is a hypersensitivity lung disease driven by an exaggerated Th2 immune response to Aspergillus fumigatus colonizing the airways of patients with asthma or cystic fibrosis. Systematic review estimates show ABPA in approximately 2.5% of adults with asthma and 7-9% of patients with cystic fibrosis, with a global burden of roughly 4-5 million cases. Patients typically present with worsening asthma despite inhaled steroids, fleeting pulmonary infiltrates, expectoration of brownish mucus plugs, marked peripheral eosinophilia, very high total IgE (often above 1,000 IU/mL), and central bronchiectasis on CT. Untreated ABPA causes progressive bronchiectasis, fibrosis, and respiratory failure. Oral prednisolone with or without itraconazole produces remission in 70-80% of patients, and newer biologics targeting IL-5 and IL-4/IL-13 are increasingly used in steroid-dependent disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2.5% of adults with asthma; 7-9% of patients with cystic fibrosis (Agarwal et al. 2009 systematic review)
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 20-50 most commonly affected; equal sex distribution; higher prevalence in South Asia, the Middle East, and parts of Europe
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of diagnosis 30-45 years; mean asthma duration before ABPA recognition 5-15 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 4-5 million people worldwide; high under-diagnosis in low- and middle-income settings

definition: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ICD-10: B44.81) is a hypersensitivity lung disease caused by an exuberant Th2 immune response to Aspergillus fumigatus that colonizes the airways without invading tissue. The condition develops in genetically and immunologically susceptible patients, predominantly those with poorly controlled atopic asthma or cystic fibrosis. Inhaled Aspergillus conidia germinate in the bronchial mucus of these patients; chronic antigen presentation drives IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 production, with B-cell IgE class switching, eosinophilic airway inflammation, mucus impaction, central bronchiectasis, and eventually fibrosis. ISHAM 2024 consensus criteria require asthma or cystic fibrosis, Aspergillus-specific IgE above 0.35 kUA/L (or positive skin prick test), and total IgE above 500 IU/mL (often >1,000 IU/mL), together with at least two of: positive Aspergillus IgG/precipitins, blood eosinophils above 500 cells/µL, and chest imaging compatible with ABPA (transient infiltrates, mucus plugging, or central bronchiectasis). Five clinical stages were defined by Patterson in 1982: acute, remission, exacerbation, corticosteroid-dependent asthma, and fibrotic end-stage. ABPA is managed by pulmonology, allergy, and infectious disease specialists, and requires lifelong monitoring because relapse is common even after successful initial treatment.

typesClassification.description: Early disease with positive immunological criteria but normal high-resolution CT of the chest. Best prognosis; corticosteroids and antifungal therapy usually achieve remission without structural damage.
Characteristic central bronchiectasis predominantly affecting the upper lobes with sparing of the periphery. Established structural disease that may progress despite treatment.
Variant with hyperdense mucus plugs on non-contrast CT (more attenuating than chest-wall muscle). Associated with severe disease, higher relapse rates, and worse outcomes.
Affects approximately 7-9% of patients with cystic fibrosis. Distinct diagnostic considerations (modified criteria) because of baseline lung disease, abnormal CT, and chronic colonization.

typesClassification.type: Patterson staging (acute, remission, exacerbation, steroid-dependent, fibrotic)
typesClassification.description: Clinical stages from initial diagnosis (acute) through remission (after 6 months off steroids), exacerbation (relapse), steroid-dependent asthma, and end-stage fibrotic disease.

primarySymptoms: Worsening asthma symptoms (wheeze, breathlessness, cough) that fail to respond to standard inhaled corticosteroid plus long-acting beta-agonist therapy.
Expectoration of thick, sticky brownish or olive-green mucus plugs, sometimes cast-like, that occasionally bring up Aspergillus hyphae on microscopy.
Recurrent low-grade fever, malaise, anorexia, and weight loss during exacerbations.
Haemoptysis from bronchiectatic airways or mucus impaction.
Chest tightness and pleuritic chest pain during pulmonary infiltrates.
Chronic productive cough with copious sputum in patients with established bronchiectasis.
Progressive exertional breathlessness and reduced exercise tolerance in advanced disease.
New or worsening fatigue not explained by asthma alone, often paralleling rising eosinophil and IgE counts.
Recurrent migratory pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray that may shift between lobes between presentations.
Symptoms despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids and biologic therapy targeting asthma, prompting investigation for ABPA.
earlyWarningSigns: Asthma that is increasingly difficult to control despite step-up therapy
Recurrent chest X-ray infiltrates in an asthmatic patient
Peripheral blood eosinophilia above 500 cells/µL in an asthma or cystic fibrosis patient
Sputum production with visible mucus plugs of brownish or rusty colour
Rising total serum IgE in a patient with known asthma without recent atopy change
Decline in lung function (FEV1) without other identifiable cause
emergencySigns: Massive haemoptysis (>200 mL in 24 hours) from bronchiectatic vessels — emergency interventional radiology and bronchial artery embolization
Acute severe asthma with poor response to standard therapy, hypoxia, and hypercapnia
Pneumothorax with sudden chest pain and breathlessness
Lobar collapse from mucus impaction with hypoxia and respiratory distress
Allergic broncho-aspergillosis evolving into invasive aspergillosis in severely immunocompromised patients (rare but life-threatening)

causes.description: ABPA is driven by a Th2-skewed immune response to A. fumigatus antigens. Inhaled conidia colonize mucus in the airways of susceptible patients; persistent antigen presentation drives IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, and high-affinity IgE production with eosinophilic inflammation and tissue remodelling.

Asthma is the most common host condition, present in 80-90% of ABPA cases. Atopic asthma with elevated total IgE provides the immunological substrate; chronic airway inflammation and mucus retention support fungal colonization.

Approximately 7-9% of patients with cystic fibrosis develop ABPA, supported by viscous airway mucus and chronic bacterial-fungal interactions. Diagnostic criteria are modified to account for baseline lung disease.

HLA-DR2 and HLA-DR5 alleles confer susceptibility. Polymorphisms in surfactant protein A, IL-4, IL-10, and the chloride channel CFTR (heterozygous status) raise risk. Polymorphisms in mannose-binding lectin and toll-like receptors influence severity.

Higher exposure to fungal spores through dampness, compost, agricultural work, or warm humid climates increases the antigenic load and probably the risk of developing ABPA. Indoor mould remediation and humidity control help reduce exposure.

riskFactors.description: Patients with severe or poorly controlled asthma have approximately 2.5% prevalence of ABPA; uncontrolled asthma raises the risk. Atopy with raised IgE is a strong predisposing background.
7-9% of CF patients develop ABPA, with viscous mucus, chronic airway colonization, and baseline atopy contributing.

riskFactors.factor: Genetic susceptibility (HLA, CFTR, IL-4, IL-10 polymorphisms)
riskFactors.description: HLA-DR2 and HLA-DR5 alleles, CFTR heterozygous status, and Th2-skewing polymorphisms confer susceptibility. Family clustering occurs occasionally.
Damp housing, basements, compost piles, agricultural work, mouldy hay, and warm humid climates increase Aspergillus inhalation and disease risk.

riskFactors.factor: Geographic location (South Asia, Middle East, parts of Europe)
riskFactors.description: Prevalence is notably higher in India and the Middle East, partly because of climate, indoor air quality, and possibly population-level atopy patterns.
Patients hospitalized with severe asthma exacerbations have a higher prevalence of ABPA; aggressive asthma management and screening for ABPA are warranted.

affectedDemographics: Adults with asthma, particularly those with severe or poorly controlled disease — about 2.5% of all asthmatics.
Cystic fibrosis patients in adolescence and adulthood — 7-9% develop ABPA.
South Asia and the Middle East have the highest reported prevalence, with India estimating 1.4 million cases.
Adults aged 20-50 represent the typical demographic; both sexes equally affected.
Patients with severe asthma referred to tertiary centres: ABPA accounts for up to 12% of cases.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical, immunological, and imaging criteria. The 2024 ISHAM/WHO consensus criteria require asthma or cystic fibrosis as the predisposing condition, plus elevated Aspergillus fumigatus-specific IgE (>0.35 kUA/L) or positive immediate-type skin prick test, and total serum IgE above 500 IU/mL (often >1,000 IU/mL); plus at least two of three: positive Aspergillus precipitins or specific IgG, blood eosinophil count above 500 cells/µL (without recent oral corticosteroid), and chest imaging compatible with ABPA (transient infiltrates, mucus plugging, central bronchiectasis). Initial work-up includes total serum IgE, A. fumigatus-specific IgE (preferred over skin prick test for quantification), Aspergillus precipitins or specific IgG, full blood count with eosinophils, sputum culture (often grows Aspergillus species), high-resolution CT of the chest, spirometry, and asthma control assessment. Distinctive HRCT findings include central bronchiectasis with sparing of distal airways, mucus impaction (the finger-in-glove sign), high-attenuation mucus on non-contrast CT (a hallmark feature), tree-in-bud opacities, and peripheral consolidation that resolves between episodes. Total IgE is the single best biomarker for monitoring; serial measurements track response and detect relapse. Differential diagnosis includes severe asthma without ABPA, eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss), chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, and bronchiectasis from other causes. Patterson staging is used at diagnosis and follow-up to guide therapy intensity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Central diagnostic and monitoring biomarker; levels typically >500 IU/mL and often >1,000 IU/mL
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 24-48 hours. Total IgE is followed every 4-6 weeks during treatment and should fall by at least 25% with remission.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates IgE sensitization to Aspergillus antigens; threshold >0.35 kUA/L
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as total IgE. Quantitative ImmunoCAP assay is preferred over skin prick test for monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects sensitization to Aspergillus through IgG response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; ELISA-based assays now preferred over older agar diffusion precipitin tests. Available at most immunology laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents eosinophilia (>500 cells/µL is criterion); high counts during exacerbation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test; results within hours. Eosinophils must be measured before starting oral corticosteroids (which suppress the count).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies central bronchiectasis, mucus impaction (finger-in-glove sign), high-attenuation mucus, and infiltrates
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contrast scan, 10-15 minutes. Performed at diagnosis and during exacerbations or if disease progression is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Aspergillus hyphae and excludes bacterial or mycobacterial infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient produces a sputum sample; culture takes 5-14 days. Aspergillus growth supports diagnosis but is not required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies airflow obstruction and tracks lung function decline
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-minute test in clinic. FEV1 typically falls during exacerbations and rises with treatment. Baseline and serial measurements guide therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates immediate-type hypersensitivity; positive in nearly all ABPA cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small drop of allergen extract on the forearm with a lancet prick; wheal read at 15 minutes. Sensitivity ≥3 mm above saline control is positive.

treatmentOverview: Treatment aims to control eosinophilic inflammation, eradicate Aspergillus colonization, prevent and treat exacerbations, and minimize long-term lung damage and corticosteroid toxicity. Oral corticosteroids remain the cornerstone of initial therapy. Most guidelines and the 2024 ISHAM consensus recommend oral prednisolone 0.5-0.75 mg/kg/day for 2-4 weeks, then tapered over 3-6 months guided by symptoms, lung function, eosinophil count, and total IgE. A randomized trial by Agarwal et al. (Chest 2018) compared medium-dose and high-dose prednisolone regimens with similar efficacy but more toxicity from high doses, supporting the medium-dose approach. Itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 16 weeks is added as an antifungal-immunomodulator; the Stevens et al. (NEJM 2000) trial showed reduced exacerbation rate and steroid sparing. Voriconazole and posaconazole are alternative options when itraconazole is poorly tolerated, with therapeutic drug monitoring. Inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists continue for asthma control. Biologics targeting type-2 inflammation are increasingly used in steroid-dependent ABPA: anti-IgE (omalizumab), anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab, reslizumab, benralizumab), and anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab) all have growing observational evidence of clinical benefit and steroid sparing. Acute exacerbations are treated with reintroduction of oral corticosteroids at the original dose and a further 4-month antifungal course. Maintenance includes annual influenza vaccination, pneumococcal vaccination, optimized inhaled therapy, sputum surveillance, and treatment of nasal polyps or chronic rhinosinusitis (which often coexist). Bronchial artery embolization is performed for life-threatening haemoptysis. Lung transplantation is reserved for end-stage fibrotic disease. Patient education includes mould exposure reduction, dampness remediation, smoking cessation, and influenza prevention.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone 0.5-0.75 mg/kg/day, tapered over 3-6 months
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for active ABPA. Induction dose for 2-4 weeks then progressive taper. Bone protection with calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonates is essential for long courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic and biochemical remission in 70-80% within 8 weeks; total IgE falls by 25-50% with response

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 16 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Antifungal-immunomodulator added to corticosteroids; reduces exacerbations and allows lower steroid doses. Monitor liver enzymes, drug levels, and interactions (CYP3A4 substrate).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces exacerbation rate by approximately 40-50% and lowers steroid dose by 30-50% in randomized trials

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole 200 mg twice daily (or weight-based) for 16 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative when itraconazole is poorly tolerated or fails. Therapeutic drug monitoring is required; watch for visual disturbance, hepatic, and dermatological adverse effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to itraconazole; supports remission in 60-70%

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroid plus long-acting beta-agonist (asthma control)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose inhaled steroid (e.g., budesonide 800 µg/day or fluticasone furoate 184 µg/day) plus formoterol or salmeterol for ongoing asthma management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves asthma control, FEV1, and quality of life but is not sufficient as monotherapy for ABPA

medicalTreatments.name: Omalizumab (anti-IgE) 150-375 mg subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in steroid-dependent ABPA with elevated IgE. Off-label dosing based on weight and pre-treatment IgE.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Steroid-sparing and reduced exacerbations in observational studies; trial evidence is emerging

medicalTreatments.name: Mepolizumab, benralizumab (anti-IL-5)
medicalTreatments.description: Approved for severe eosinophilic asthma; used off-label in ABPA with persistent eosinophilia. Mepolizumab 100 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks; benralizumab 30 mg every 4-8 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces eosinophilia and exacerbation rate in case series and open-label studies

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) 300 mg subcutaneously every 2 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Targets IL-4 and IL-13 signalling; used in severe eosinophilic asthma and ABPA refractory to other therapy. Case reports show steroid sparing and lung function improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Encouraging in observational data; trials ongoing

medicalTreatments.name: Bone protection (calcium 1,000 mg, vitamin D 800-1,000 IU, bisphosphonate when on long-term steroids)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard prophylaxis for patients on prednisolone >5 mg for over 3 months. Reduces vertebral fracture risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-70% reduction in fracture risk in glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis trials

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchoscopic mucus plug removal
surgicalOptions.description: Therapeutic bronchoscopy with suction and forceps removal of impacted mucus in segmental or lobar collapse. Performed under sedation. Repeated if necessary.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Re-expansion of collapsed lung in 80-90% of cases; symptomatic improvement is usually rapid

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchial artery embolization
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency interventional radiology procedure for massive or recurrent haemoptysis. Catheter-based embolization of bleeding bronchial arteries.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial control of haemoptysis in 80-90%; rebleeding rate 10-30% within 1-2 years

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for end-stage fibrotic ABPA with respiratory failure refractory to medical therapy. Carries high perioperative risk and lifelong immunosuppression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival 50-60% in published series; long-term outcomes complicated by chronic rejection

alternativeTreatments.name: Mould exposure reduction (home remediation, dehumidifiers, HEPA filtration)
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing fungal antigen load in damp homes lowers airway exposure. Address basements, leaks, and indoor humidity above 50%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercise, breathing technique, and education programme for patients with established bronchiectasis or chronic disease. Improves exercise capacity and quality of life.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist or allergist with ABPA experience is needed to confirm the diagnosis, design corticosteroid and antifungal regimens, manage drug interactions and toxicity, and decide on biologic therapy. Multidisciplinary input from cystic fibrosis teams, ENT for sinusitis, and interventional radiology for haemoptysis is essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with severe asthma or bronchiectasis specialist clinic, high-resolution CT capability, therapeutic drug monitoring for antifungals, and access to biologic therapies. Ask about exacerbation rates, biologic experience, and policies on prolonged steroid use.
hospitalCriteria: Severe asthma or interstitial lung disease specialist clinic
High-resolution CT and respiratory radiology expertise
Therapeutic drug monitoring for itraconazole and voriconazole
Pulmonary function laboratory with bronchoprovocation capability
Access to bronchial artery embolization and bronchoscopy
keyFacilities: Adult and paediatric cystic fibrosis clinics
preventionStrategies: Achieve and maintain good asthma or cystic fibrosis control with adherent inhaled therapy to reduce mucus retention and fungal colonization.
Remediate damp housing, fix leaks, and maintain indoor humidity below 50%.
Use HEPA air filtration in the bedroom for sensitized patients.
Avoid occupational mould exposure (compost, hay, agricultural settings) when possible; use respiratory protection if unavoidable.
Stop smoking and avoid second-hand smoke to protect lung function.
Screen asthma patients with poor control and CF patients annually with total IgE and Aspergillus-specific IgE.
Vaccinate annually against influenza and complete pneumococcal vaccination schedule.
lifestyleModifications: Take asthma controller therapy daily and use a written action plan with peak flow monitoring.
Avoid known mould exposure (basements, compost piles, mouldy hay) and wear an N95 respirator when unavoidable.
Maintain a balanced diet rich in fresh fruit, vegetables, oily fish, and whole grains.
Exercise regularly with moderate aerobic activity and pulmonary rehabilitation when bronchiectasis is established.
Stop smoking permanently and avoid second-hand smoke.
Perform daily airway clearance (Acapella, active cycle of breathing) for patients with bronchiectasis.
Limit alcohol while on antifungal therapy because of liver interactions.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, olive oil, and whole grains
Adequate calcium (1,000 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1,000 IU/day) for bone protection on long-term steroids
Protein-rich diet (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support immune function and prevent steroid myopathy
Moderate intake of probiotic-rich foods (yoghurt, kefir) to support gut health on antifungals
avoid: Mouldy or visibly fermented foods that can increase airborne Aspergillus exposure
Grapefruit juice while on itraconazole or voriconazole (significant CYP3A4 interaction)
Heavy alcohol consumption while on antifungals (hepatotoxicity risk)
Excess added sugars and saturated fats that worsen steroid-induced metabolic side effects

exerciseGuidelines: Encourage 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly. Pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for patients with established bronchiectasis or fibrosis. Avoid swimming in untreated freshwater because of fungal contamination risk. Resume activity gradually after exacerbations.
dailyManagement: Take oral steroids in the morning with food, maintaining the tapering schedule.
Take itraconazole capsules with a meal or acidic drink to enhance absorption (oral solution preferred when taken on empty stomach).
Use inhaled controller therapy every morning and evening, and a reliever as needed.
Monitor peak expiratory flow daily and record values in a chart.
Watch for warning signs of exacerbation (increased breathlessness, sputum colour change, fever) and contact specialist promptly.
Maintain hydration to keep airway mucus thin and aid expectoration.
Avoid known fungal exposures and use respiratory protection in dusty or mouldy environments.
prognosis: With early diagnosis and treatment, 70-80% of patients achieve clinical remission and many remain stable for years. However, relapse rates are 30-50% within 5 years, and 20-30% of patients become steroid-dependent. Long-term outcomes depend on the stage at diagnosis: ABPA-S has the best prognosis with often complete resolution; ABPA-CB shows fixed bronchiectasis but stable lung function with treatment; ABPA-CB-HAM and fibrotic ABPA have higher exacerbation rates and progressive lung function decline. Lung function (FEV1) declines on average 1-2% per year in well-treated ABPA but faster in those with frequent exacerbations. Mortality is uncommon but rises in patients with established fibrosis, respiratory failure, or complications such as massive haemoptysis. Biologic therapy is changing the long-term outlook for steroid-dependent disease, with case series showing sustained remission and substantial corticosteroid sparing. Total IgE remains the best biomarker for monitoring; a sustained rise (>50% above remission baseline) is the earliest sign of relapse.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms typically improve within 1-2 weeks of starting oral steroids. Eosinophils normalize within 1-2 weeks. Total IgE falls 25-50% over 6-8 weeks of treatment. Imaging infiltrates resolve over 1-3 months. Many patients require 3-6 months of tapering steroid therapy. Bronchiectasis, once established, is permanent but progression can be slowed.
complications: Chronic bronchiectasis with daily sputum production and recurrent bacterial infections — managed with airway clearance and antibiotics.
Pulmonary fibrosis and end-stage lung disease — develops in long-standing inadequately treated ABPA; may require oxygen and lung transplantation.
Massive haemoptysis from bronchiectatic vessels — requires emergency bronchial artery embolization.
Recurrent pneumonias and bacterial superinfection of bronchiectasis (Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus).
Pneumothorax from rupture of bullae or cyst formation.
Corticosteroid toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataract, infection) — prevent with bone protection, glucose monitoring, ophthalmology review.
Itraconazole toxicity (hepatotoxicity, drug interactions, heart failure) — monitor liver function and drug levels.

supportResources.name: Asthma + Lung UK — Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information, helpline, and support for ABPA.

supportResources.name: Aspergillosis Trust (Manchester, UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient organisation focused on aspergillosis, including ABPA, with patient stories and clinical updates.

supportResources.name: Cystic Fibrosis Foundation — ABPA in CF
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with resources on ABPA in cystic fibrosis.

supportResources.name: Aspergillus Website (LIFE programme)
supportResources.description: Comprehensive professional and patient resource on aspergillus infections including ABPA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma (without ABPA)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma alone responds to inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators without high IgE, mucus plug expectoration, or central bronchiectasis. ABPA features total IgE >500 IU/mL, Aspergillus-specific IgE, and characteristic central bronchiectasis on CT; all severe or poorly controlled asthma should be screened.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bronchiectasis (other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bronchiectasis from infection, immunodeficiency, or post-tuberculosis affects peripheral airways and lacks the high eosinophilia and IgE of ABPA. ABPA produces predominantly central bronchiectasis with sparing of the periphery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cystic Fibrosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disease causing thick mucus, recurrent infection, and pancreatic insufficiency. ABPA can complicate cystic fibrosis (7-9% prevalence) and is diagnosed with modified criteria. Sweat chloride and CFTR genotyping distinguish the underlying CF.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB causes constitutional symptoms, cavitary lung disease, sputum acid-fast bacilli, and constitutional weight loss; treatment is antitubercular drugs. ABPA mimics include cavitary disease and infiltrates but features eosinophilia and high IgE; sputum culture rules out TB.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying condition in 80-90% of ABPA cases; severe or poorly controlled asthma should be screened with total IgE and Aspergillus-specific IgE.

relatedConditions.name: Bronchiectasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Central bronchiectasis is a hallmark of established ABPA; airway clearance and infection prevention are integral to care.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis is the upper-airway counterpart of ABPA; often coexists and benefits from combined ENT-respiratory management.

relatedConditions.name: Cystic Fibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: ABPA complicates 7-9% of CF cases; recognized as a leading cause of pulmonary deterioration in adolescent and adult CF.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal Polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps overlaps clinically and pathophysiologically with ABPA.

faqs.question: What is ABPA?
faqs.answer: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) is a hypersensitivity lung disease caused by an allergic reaction to Aspergillus fumigatus colonizing the airways of patients with asthma or cystic fibrosis. Symptoms include worsening asthma, mucus plug expectoration, fever, and migratory pulmonary infiltrates.

faqs.question: Who gets ABPA?
faqs.answer: ABPA affects approximately 2.5% of adults with asthma and 7-9% of patients with cystic fibrosis. Risk is highest in atopic patients with poorly controlled asthma, those with severe asthma, and patients with environmental exposure to Aspergillus spores.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of ABPA?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include worsening asthma despite inhaled steroids, expectoration of brownish mucus plugs, low-grade fever, haemoptysis, productive cough, and recurrent migratory pulmonary infiltrates on chest imaging. Many patients have years of difficult-to-control asthma before diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is ABPA diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines asthma or cystic fibrosis as a host condition, elevated Aspergillus-specific IgE, total serum IgE above 500 IU/mL, plus at least two of positive Aspergillus precipitins, eosinophilia above 500 cells/µL, and CT findings of central bronchiectasis or mucus impaction.

faqs.question: What does ABPA look like on CT?
faqs.answer: Characteristic CT findings include central bronchiectasis with peripheral sparing, mucus impaction (the finger-in-glove sign), high-attenuation mucus plugs, tree-in-bud opacities, and migratory peripheral consolidation. High-attenuation mucus is highly specific for ABPA.

faqs.question: How is ABPA treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is oral prednisolone 0.5-0.75 mg/kg/day for 2-4 weeks tapered over 3-6 months, with itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 16 weeks as steroid-sparing antifungal therapy. Biologics targeting IgE, IL-5, or IL-4/IL-13 are used in steroid-dependent disease.

faqs.question: Can ABPA be cured?
faqs.answer: ABPA is a chronic relapsing condition rather than a curable infection. Most patients achieve remission with treatment, but 30-50% relapse within 5 years. Long-term outcomes depend on early diagnosis, adherence, and lifestyle changes including mould exposure reduction.

faqs.question: Is ABPA contagious?
faqs.answer: No. ABPA is an allergic response to inhaled Aspergillus spores and is not transmitted from person to person. Aspergillus spores are common in the environment, but only susceptible individuals (with asthma or cystic fibrosis) develop the disease.

faqs.question: What is total IgE in ABPA?
faqs.answer: Total serum IgE is markedly elevated in ABPA, typically above 500 IU/mL and often above 1,000 IU/mL. It is the single best biomarker for diagnosis and monitoring; a 25-50% fall during treatment indicates remission and a 50% rise from baseline often signals relapse.

faqs.question: Are antifungals enough on their own?
faqs.answer: Antifungals alone are usually insufficient as first-line therapy; oral corticosteroids are the cornerstone for controlling eosinophilic inflammation. Itraconazole or voriconazole is added to reduce exacerbations and lower the steroid dose, especially in patients with frequent relapse.

faqs.question: Can biologics treat ABPA?
faqs.answer: Yes. Omalizumab (anti-IgE), mepolizumab and benralizumab (anti-IL-5), and dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) are increasingly used in steroid-dependent ABPA. Observational evidence shows reduced exacerbations, steroid sparing, and improved lung function. Randomized trials are ongoing.

faqs.question: Does ABPA cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Long-standing or recurrent ABPA can cause permanent bronchiectasis and, in advanced cases, pulmonary fibrosis. Early diagnosis and adequate treatment slow or prevent structural progression. Patients with high-attenuation mucus or established bronchiectasis at diagnosis have worse long-term outcomes.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy in ABPA?
faqs.answer: Most patients with treated ABPA have near-normal life expectancy. Those with end-stage fibrosis, respiratory failure, or major haemoptysis have higher mortality. Coexisting cystic fibrosis is the main determinant of overall survival in CF patients with ABPA.

faqs.question: How long do you take steroids for ABPA?
faqs.answer: A typical course is 3-6 months of prednisolone with progressive tapering. Patients with relapse or steroid dependence may need longer or alternative agents (itraconazole, biologics) to limit cumulative steroid toxicity.

faqs.question: Can I prevent ABPA?
faqs.answer: Risk reduction includes optimal asthma or cystic fibrosis control, indoor humidity below 50%, mould remediation in damp homes, HEPA filtration, avoidance of compost and mouldy hay, and respiratory protection in occupational exposure. Annual screening with total IgE in severe asthma helps early diagnosis.

faqs.question: Does ABPA affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. ABPA can present in adolescents and young adults, especially those with cystic fibrosis. Symptoms include difficult-to-treat asthma, mucus plug expectoration, and recurrent infiltrates. Diagnostic criteria and treatment are similar to adults.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ABPA and aspergilloma?
faqs.answer: ABPA is an allergic disease driven by hypersensitivity to Aspergillus in the airways of asthma or CF patients. Aspergilloma is a fungal ball that grows in pre-existing lung cavities (e.g., post-TB), causing haemoptysis. Both involve Aspergillus but are pathologically and clinically distinct.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with ABPA?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular moderate exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) is encouraged. Pulmonary rehabilitation is beneficial for patients with established bronchiectasis. Avoid mouldy environments and untreated freshwater. Pace activity during exacerbations and resume gradually after recovery.

faqs.question: Does diet affect ABPA?
faqs.answer: A Mediterranean-style diet supports overall health; calcium, vitamin D, and protein are important during long-term steroid therapy. Avoid grapefruit juice on itraconazole or voriconazole. Limit alcohol because of antifungal hepatotoxicity risk.

faqs.question: How often should ABPA be monitored?
faqs.answer: Patients are followed every 6-12 weeks during active treatment with total IgE, lung function, and symptom review. Once in remission, monitoring extends to 3-6 monthly. Total IgE rising more than 50% above baseline often signals relapse.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested?
faqs.answer: Routine screening of family members is not recommended unless they have asthma, cystic fibrosis, or recurrent unexplained respiratory disease. Genetic predisposition is modest; environmental exposure is a major contributor.

metaDescription: ABPA (allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis): high IgE asthma, central bronchiectasis, steroids, itraconazole, biologics. Medically reviewed guide.
